common_name: Fragrant Water Lily (American White Water Lily)

planting.note: Plant rhizome sections in spring in aquatic planting containers filled with heavy, nutrient-rich clay-based soil submerged 1–2 feet below the water surface in full sun; fragrant water lily is a native North American aquatic plant outstanding for garden ponds, water features, and naturalized wetland gardens from zone 3 through zone 11.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in heavy clay-loam soil in an aquatic basket set 12–24 inches below the water surface in full sun; do not use organic potting mix. Nymphaea odorata (fragrant water lily or American white water lily) is the native water lily of eastern North America — one of the most beautiful and ecologically important aquatic plants of ponds, lakes, and slow-moving waters from Newfoundland to Florida and Texas. It produces the classic, drifting, rounded floating leaves (lily pads) 4–12 inches across and exquisitely beautiful, cupped, multi-petaled white flowers 2–5 inches across with golden-yellow stamens, opening in the morning and closing by midday, with an intensely sweet vanilla-rose fragrance carried over the water surface on warm summer mornings. The flowers are highly attractive to native bees and other pollinators that visit while the flowers are open. The floating leaves provide surface shade that reduces algae growth and provides refuge for fish and aquatic insects. The plant is vigorous and will spread readily by rhizome in natural pond settings — excellent for naturalizing wetland margins and rain gardens. Young buds, seeds, and roots were used as food by many Native American peoples. Grow in full sun for maximum flowering.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aquatic Plants

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring in moist to wet soil or in shallow water (2–12 inches deep) at pond margins, rain gardens, or wet ditches in full sun; southern cattail is a warm-climate native cattail adapted to the Gulf Coastal Plain and is the dominant cattail species of southern marshes, ditches, and shorelines.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in moist to wet soil or shallow water at pond or wetland margins in full sun; southern cattail spreads aggressively by rhizome and may be invasive in small water features — best for large ponds or natural wetland restoration. Typha domingensis (southern cattail) is the dominant native cattail of the Gulf Coastal Plain, replacing common cattail (T. latifolia) in warm-climate marshes and ditches from the Carolinas through Florida and Texas. It is distinguished from common cattail by its narrower leaves (hence 'narrow-leaf cattail') and the characteristic gap between the female and male portions of the cigar-shaped flower spike — the two sections are separated by a visible gap in T. domingensis, while they are contiguous in T. latifolia. Cattails are among the most important edible wild plants of temperate and subtropical North America: the young shoots (peeled) are eaten like asparagus in spring; the green flower spikes are boiled and eaten like corn on the cob; the pollen is collected as a golden flour rich in protein; the starchy rhizomes can be processed into flour. They also provide outstanding wildlife habitat — nesting material and cover for numerous marsh bird species, seeds for waterfowl, and dense stands shelter fish.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aquatic Plants
USDA PLANTS Database — Typha domingensis

planting.note: Plant grafted stock in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, deep soil in full sun; blood oranges require a cool autumn to develop their characteristic red pigmentation (anthocyanins) — in zone 9, fall night temperatures below 60°F trigger the color development; without cool nights, the flesh remains orange.

directions: Plant grafted stock in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; fertilize with citrus formula in late winter, late spring, and early fall. Citrus × sinensis 'Moro' (Moro blood orange) is the most intensely red-fleshed blood orange variety — a Sicilian mutation of sweet orange in which the flesh, juice, and sometimes the rind develop stunning deep-red to near-purple color from anthocyanin pigments produced in response to cool autumn nights. The 'Moro' cultivar produces the deepest, most intensely colored blood-red flesh and dramatic dark-red juice of any blood orange, with a distinctive flavor that combines sweet orange with a raspberry-berry overtone from the anthocyanins. In zone 9, the cool fall and winter nights (below 60°F) trigger the red pigment development; in warmer climates the color development is minimal. Blood orange juice makes extraordinary cocktails, salad dressings, and sauces. The 'Tarocco' variety (less cold-hardy, zone 10) produces the best flavor; 'Sanguinelli' is another reliable variety. Blood oranges ripen December through March in zone 9.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blood Oranges
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Home Citrus
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Citrus Production

common_name: Hardy Orange (Trifoliate Orange, Japanese Bitter Orange)

planting.note: Plant container or bare-root stock in spring in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; trifoliate orange is the most cold-hardy citrus relative, surviving temperatures to -10°F when dormant — it is the primary rootstock for grafting other citrus species and an important cold-climate curiosity.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moist, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; hardy orange is deciduous (unusual for citrus) and leafless in winter. Poncirus trifoliata (trifoliate orange or hardy orange) is the most cold-hardy member of the citrus family — a deciduous, thorny Chinese shrub or small tree that survives temperatures to -10°F when dormant, making it the only citrus relative that can be grown permanently outdoors through USDA zone 5. The small, 1.5-to-2-inch, round, yellow fruits resemble a tiny orange but are intensely bitter and unpleasant raw (high limonin content) — technically edible as marmalade or preserves if the bitterness is accounted for, but not for fresh eating. The plant's primary horticultural importance is as a dwarfing, cold-hardy rootstock for grafting edible citrus — trifoliate orange rootstock limits tree size, improves cold hardiness, and increases fruit quality in grafted sweet oranges, grapefruits, and mandarins. As an ornamental, it is a thorny barrier or specimen plant with fragrant white flowers in spring (before the leaves emerge), attractive yellow fall fruits, and interesting green-barked, zig-zag winter branch structure. A novel cold-climate citrus curiosity.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rootstocks for Citrus
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder
Purdue University — Trifoliate Orange

common_name: Chinaberry Tree (Bead Tree, Pride of India)

planting.note: Plant container stock or direct sow seeds in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to moderately moist soil in full sun; chinaberry grows extremely rapidly (5–10 feet per year) and is widely naturalized across the Gulf South — be aware it is invasive in some states.

directions: Plant in any well-drained, moist to dry soil in full sun; chinaberry grows in almost any condition and requires minimal care — it is extremely fast-growing. CAUTION: Invasive in the southeastern United States — verify local regulations before planting. Melia azedarach (chinaberry or bead tree) is an Asian tree that has naturalized across the entire Gulf Coastal Plain and has been a fixture of southern rural landscapes for generations, valued for its extraordinary fast growth (5–10 feet per year), deep, cooling shade, and clusters of fragrant, lilac-purple flowers in April–May that make it one of the most ornamentally beautiful trees in bloom despite its weedy reputation. The clusters of small, round, yellow-white berries persist into winter and are eagerly consumed by American Robins and other fruit-eating birds, which has facilitated its spread. CAUTION: The berries are toxic to humans (causing gastrointestinal distress) and especially to pigs; all parts of the plant contain triterpenoids and limonoids. Chinaberry is listed as invasive in Florida, Georgia, and much of the Gulf South. Historically important as a fast-growing 'pioneer tree' for farmstead windbreaks and shade, and the hard berries have been used for rosary beads and crafts (giving it the 'bead tree' name).
sources: USDA PLANTS Database — Melia azedarach
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Trees
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tree Identification

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; wingleaf soapberry is native to rocky limestone soils, coastal chaparral, and sandy plains of southern Texas through Mexico and is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; soapberry is drought-tolerant once established and thrives in poor, alkaline soils. Sapindus saponaria (wingleaf soapberry or wild China tree) is a native Texas, Caribbean, and tropical American tree producing clusters of small, round, translucent, amber-gold berries in fall that contain saponins — the natural soap compounds that give the genus its Latin name (Sapo = soap, Indus = Indian, originally referring to 'soap nut from the New World'). When the berry flesh is worked with water, it creates a genuine, biodegradable, soap-like lather that has been used by indigenous peoples and settlers for washing clothes, hair, and skin for centuries; the berries are the same 'soapnuts' sold in natural products stores as a biodegradable laundry detergent. The tree is a handsome, medium-sized shade tree with pinnately compound leaves, small white flowers, and attractive amber berries that persist into winter. The hard, black seeds inside the berries have traditionally been used as beads. Outstanding for low-maintenance, drought-tolerant landscaping in the Gulf South, especially on alkaline soils.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Trees
USDA Forest Service — Sapindus saponaria

common_name: New Mexico Privet (Desert Olive, Elbow Bush)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, caliche, or sandy soil in full sun to part shade; New Mexico privet is native to dry, rocky limestone hillsides and arroyos of the Chihuahuan Desert, Trans-Pecos Texas, and adjacent New Mexico and Oklahoma — it is extremely drought-tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; once established, no supplemental irrigation is needed. Forestiera pubescens (New Mexico privet or desert olive) is a native Texas and New Mexico shrub — a member of the olive family (Oleaceae) producing olive-like, small (1/4-inch), blue-black drupes in fall that are eagerly consumed by migrating and resident birds, including mockingbirds, robins, and waxwings. The shrub is a multi-stemmed, densely branching, semi-evergreen plant with small, light-green, opposite leaves and tiny, inconspicuous, yellowish flowers in early spring before the leaves emerge. As a landscape plant, Forestiera pubescens is valued for extreme drought and limestone tolerance, dense wildlife-supporting structure, and use as a native hedge or screen plant that requires no supplemental water once established. It is an outstanding alternative to non-native privets (Ligustrum species) in dry southwestern and southern landscapes, providing equivalent screening value with native habitat benefits. The small, clustered fruits produced in fall are an important berry food source for fall migrants moving through the southwestern plains.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Shrubs
New Mexico State University Extension — Native Plants
